---
title: "Intro to CoreHPC"
subtitle: "Part 1"
author: "Natalie Gill"
institute: "Gladstone Bioinformatics Core"
date: "TBD"
date-format: MMMM DD, YYYY
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: corehpc-theme.scss
    highlight: default
    code-line-numbers: false
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
---

```{r, setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(comment = "")
```

##

<center>*Press the ? key for tips on navigating these slides*</center>

## Introductions


&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;**Natalie Gill**
&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;*Bioinformatician II*

## Target Audience

-   Prior experience with UNIX command-line
-   No prior experience on computing clusters

## Part 1:

1.  What is CoreHPC?
2.  Accessing CoreHPC
3.  Storage
4.  Data Transfer
5.  Software with OpenHPC Modules
6.  Python Environments with Miniforge3
7.  Containers


# What is CoreHPC?

## Overview {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: institutional HPC for UCSF, originally AI/Data Science focus, expanding to general HPC -->

## Architecture

![](slide_materials/CoreHPC_diagram.svg){fig-alt="CoreHPC architecture diagram showing bastion nodes, login node, compute nodes, and Globus endpoints" .nostretch fig-align="center" width=80%}

## Partitions {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: CPU, GPU, Pod, Community CPU partitions and what each is for -->

## Hardware Highlights {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: brief node specs (cores, RAM, GPU types). Note SLURM partition names -->

## Access Model {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: PI-requested accounts, default allocations (100k core hr, 1440 GPU hr), Pod by request, condo buy-in -->


# Accessing CoreHPC

## Prerequisites {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: UCSF campus account, CoreHPC-provisioned account, UCSFwpa or VPN, MFA/DUO -->

## Step 1: SSH to a Bastion Node

<!-- TODO: ssh <user>@chpc-ucsf-bastion-vm1.corehpc.ucsf.edu, alternate vm2, MFA challenge -->

## Step 2: Hop to the Login Node

<!-- TODO: from bastion: ssh chpc-ucsf-login-vm1 -->

## The Login Node {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: what it's for (job submission, staging data), what NOT to do (heavy compute), the one node with internet access -->

## The Bastion Nodes

<!-- TODO: gateway only, no compute, scp/rsync currently blocked (Globus required soon) -->


# Storage

## Storage Layout {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: home (20 GB), /mnt/scratch/user/$USER (50 TB, 30-day cleanup), /mnt/scratch/group/<FAC>, FAC capacity storage ($52/TB/yr), /mnt/lscratch on compute nodes -->

## Home Directory

<!-- TODO: 20 GB quota, shared across login/compute (not bastion), good for scripts/configs -->

## Scratch {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: /mnt/scratch limits, 30-day policy, 50 TB user cap, when to use vs FAC -->

## FAC Capacity Storage {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: project storage, recharge model, how to request (https://tiny.ucsf.edu/getfac), CXyyyyyy_DSy naming -->

## Storage Advice {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: scratch is volatile, use FAC for persistent data, avoid metadata-heavy IO patterns -->


# Data Transfer

## Transfer Options {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: Globus (recommended), FAC share cp/rsync from login node, temporary admin bastion scp for early access users -->

## Globus {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: globus.org login with UCSF CILogon, "UCSF CoreHPC - User Home" and "User Scratch" endpoints, Globus Connect Personal for local machine -->

## FAC Share Copy

<!-- TODO: cp -R /mnt/fac/<share>/<file> /mnt/scratch/group/<share>/ example -->

## Hands-on

<!-- TODO: small Globus transfer exercise -->


# Software with OpenHPC Modules

## Module Basics {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: software is provided via OpenHPC modules, consistent across login and compute nodes -->

## Common Commands

<!-- TODO: module avail, module load <name>, module list, module unload, module --help -->

## Example Available Modules {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: paste sample module avail output (apptainer, cuda, gnu14, miniforge3, nvidia/nvhpc, openmpi5, etc.) -->


# Python Environments with Miniforge3

## What is Miniforge? {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: minimal conda distribution, conda-forge default channel, why use envs -->

## Setup (Once) {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: module load miniforge3, conda init bash, redirect envs_dirs and pkgs_dirs to /scratch/user/$USER to avoid 20 GB home quota -->

## Creating an Environment

<!-- TODO: conda create -n project1 python=3.11, conda activate, conda install -->

## Exporting an Environment

<!-- TODO: conda env export > environment.yml, conda env create -f environment.yml -->


# Containers

## Motivation {.small-bullets}

<!-- TODO: why containers (reproducibility, OS isolation, hard-to-install tools) -->

## Apptainer on CoreHPC

<!-- TODO: module load apptainer, apptainer pull docker://..., apptainer exec/run/shell -->

## Example: Pull a Container

<!-- TODO: apptainer pull docker://natalie23gill/hello-world:1.0 (from login node, which has internet) -->


# Polls

## Poll 1

<!-- TODO: which node should you run heavy compute on? (answer: not the login node, use sbatch / salloc) -->

## Poll 2

<!-- TODO: scratch retention question - True/False the 30-day cleanup -->


# End of Part 1

## Workshop survey

- If you are not attending part 2, please fill out our [workshop survey](TBD) so we can continue to improve these workshops

## Upcoming Data Science Training Program Workshops {.smaller}

<!-- TODO: fill in upcoming workshop list near the workshop date -->
